raw_text: I was working in my field on the southern edge of Sidi Marzouq on the morning of March 12th. Around 6:30 in the morning, I heard the sound of heavy vehicles coming from the north road. I saw three military trucks and one armoured vehicle enter the village. Within thirty minutes, I could see thick black smoke rising from the eastern part of the village. I later learned that the primary school and at least seven houses were set on fire. I saw soldiers in dark green uniforms moving through the streets. They were carrying weapons and shouting at people to leave. I ran home, collected my family — my wife and three children — and we left through the western fields. On the way to El Mansourah, which is about twelve kilometres away, we passed through the southern farmland and saw that crops had been burned too. I estimate at least forty to fifty people were walking with us in the same direction. I still have my identity documents that I grabbed before leaving. We arrived in El Mansourah around noon.
